title: Iranian Drones Hit AWS Data Centers, SpaceX Buys Cursor for $60B, Alibaba Open-Sources CUDA Rival
subhead: Iran confirmed drone strikes on three Amazon cloud facilities in the Gulf, turning regional conflict into digital infrastructure risk. SpaceX deepened its AI pivot by acquiring coding startup Cursor for $60 billion, and Alibaba's chip unit T-Head open-sourced its full software stack to challenge Nvidia's CUDA monopoly.

sections.heading: Iranian Drone Strikes Hit Three AWS Data Centers in Gulf
sections.body:
Iranian drone strikes hit three AWS data centers — two in the UAE and one in Bahrain — marking the first confirmed state attacks on commercial cloud infrastructure. According to Tom's Hardware, Iran explicitly stated it was targeting Amazon's cloud infrastructure, and the strikes caused service disruptions across the Gulf region. AWS has not disclosed the full extent of damage or outage duration.

A CSIS analysis published this week examines how the Iran conflict is costing joint Gulf-US AI ambitions. Gulf states that invested heavily in US-partnered data center projects — including the UAE's multi-billion-dollar AI infrastructure deals — are now questioning whether hosting American cloud infrastructure makes them targets rather than beneficiaries. The strikes validate longstanding concerns that data centers are soft targets in modern conflict, and that the 'sovereign AI' buildout in the Gulf carries real physical risk.
sections.whyItMatters: State attacks on commercial cloud infrastructure are a new escalation. The Gulf's data center boom was supposed to diversify oil-dependent economies; instead it may have made them military targets.

sections.heading: SpaceX Acquires Cursor for $60 Billion, Musk Becomes First Trillionaire
sections.body:
SpaceX is officially buying AI coding startup Cursor for $60 billion, according to The Verge. The deal is one of the largest tech acquisitions in history and signals SpaceX's strategic shift from a space launch and satellite company into an AI-integrated platform. Cursor, which makes AI-powered coding tools, will reportedly operate as an independent division within SpaceX.

The acquisition comes amid SpaceX's IPO — now trading publicly, though below its initial offering price. The Verge reports that Elon Musk has become the world's first trillionaire, with his combined stakes in SpaceX, Tesla, xAI, and other ventures crossing the $1 trillion mark. An accompanying Verge feature examines the ethical implications of that milestone, noting the concentration of wealth and power in a single figure whose companies span space, social media, AI, and automotive industries.
sections.whyItMatters: A space company buying a coding AI startup for $60B redefines what SpaceX is. The vertical integration — launch capacity, satellite network, and now AI development tools — creates an unprecedented technology conglomerate.

sections.heading: Alibaba T-Head Open-Sources Its CUDA Rival
sections.body:
Alibaba's chip design unit T-Head open-sourced its full software stack, including the runtime, compiler, and development tools for its Xuantie processors, according to SCMP. The move is a direct challenge to Nvidia's CUDA ecosystem, which has been the de facto standard for AI development software for over a decade.

The open-sourcing comes at a moment when CUDA's dominance is under pressure on multiple fronts. US export controls have made Nvidia hardware harder to obtain in China, pushing domestic chipmakers to build alternatives — but the software ecosystem has always been the harder problem. T-Head's move lowers that barrier by giving developers a free, modifiable alternative to build on. If it gains traction, it could accelerate China's decoupling from Nvidia not just in hardware but in the developer ecosystem that locks customers in.
sections.whyItMatters: CUDA's moat has always been software, not hardware. Open-sourcing a viable alternative removes the biggest barrier to leaving Nvidia's ecosystem — and timing it alongside export controls makes adoption more likely.

sections.heading: Xi Jinping Addresses World AI Conference, Calls for 'Openness'
sections.body:
Xi Jinping delivered his first-ever keynote address at the World AI Conference (WAIC) in Shanghai, calling for AI 'openness' and opposing 'one country's dominance' over the technology, according to SCMP. The speech was carefully calibrated: positioned against US export controls that restrict China's access to advanced chips, Xi's 'openness' framing reframes the tech competition as a choice between shared development and gatekeeping.

SCMP also reports that the conference showcased humanoid robots, autonomous AI agents, and Huawei consumer devices — a deliberate display of China's domestic AI capabilities despite chip restrictions. Moonshot AI used the venue to launch Kimi K3, which the company claims beats US rivals on several benchmarks at lower cost, per Nikkei Asia.
sections.whyItMatters: Xi's personal appearance at WAIC signals the political priority of AI. The 'openness' pitch is aimed at the Global South — positioning China as the inclusive alternative to a gatekeeping West.

sections.heading: Japan Forms AI Consortium with Nvidia Rubin Chips, Markets Plunge on AI Deleverage
sections.body:
SoftBank, Sony, and Honda are spearheading a Japan AI consortium that will build domestic AI infrastructure using 27,500 of Nvidia's next-generation Rubin chips, according to Nikkei Asia. The project represents one of the largest single Rubin deployments announced and is designed to give Japan sovereign AI compute capacity rather than relying on US cloud providers.

In a sharp contrast, Japanese markets are reeling. The Nikkei plunged 4,100 points in a single session amid an AI deleverage selloff, with Kioxia falling 16%, according to Nikkei Asia. The selloff reflects growing concern that AI infrastructure spending has outpaced near-term revenue expectations, and that the capital-intensive chip and data center buildout may not deliver returns quickly enough.

Separately, Rapidus partnered with Cadence on AI agent-driven chip design tools, aiming to accelerate Japan's advanced semiconductor manufacturing timeline, Nikkei Asia reports.
sections.whyItMatters: Japan is simultaneously betting billions on AI compute sovereignty and watching its markets question whether the bet will pay off. The tension between sovereign AI investment and market confidence is not unique to Japan — but the scale of the swing is.

sections.heading: Apple Sues OpenAI for Trade Secret Theft
sections.body:
Apple has filed a lawsuit against OpenAI alleging trade secret theft, according to The Verge. The suit claims that former Apple employees who joined OpenAI (and its hardware subsidiary IO Products) took proprietary information about Apple's hardware and silicon design. Apple alleges the stolen trade secrets relate to custom chip architecture and hardware-AI integration work that OpenAI and IO Products are now using to build their own devices.

This is the latest escalation in a rapidly fragmenting relationship between Apple and OpenAI, which began as a partnership (Apple integrating ChatGPT into iOS) and has since deteriorated into direct competition. OpenAI's hardware ambitions through IO Products put it on a collision course with Apple's core business.
sections.whyItMatters: A trade secret lawsuit between two of the world's most valuable tech companies over AI hardware talent is a sign that the AI stack competition has moved from software into silicon — and that poaching engineers now carries legal risk.

sections.heading: Anthropic Commits $50 Billion to US AI Data Centers
sections.body:
Anthropic announced a $50 billion investment in US-based AI data center infrastructure, according to The Verge. The commitment significantly exceeds the company's previous infrastructure plans and signals that Anthropic is moving from a model-training company into a compute platform operator.

The investment comes amid broader industry debate about whether AI companies should own their own infrastructure or rely on cloud providers. Anthropic's decision to build rather than rent suggests it wants control over its compute pipeline — from training to inference — at a time when GPU supply constraints and geopolitical risk make relying on AWS, Azure, or Google Cloud a strategic vulnerability.
sections.whyItMatters: Anthropic's $50B bet on owning infrastructure means the AI lab landscape is bifurcating: some companies rent compute, others build it. The question is whether vertical integration justifies the capital outlay — or whether it recreates the same concentration of power that open-source advocates are trying to break.

bottomLine: Iran's drone strikes on AWS data centers turned the Gulf's AI infrastructure boom into a military liability. SpaceX's $60B Cursor acquisition and Anthropic's $50B data center commitment show AI companies racing to own the full stack — from launch pads to coding tools to silicon. Alibaba's open-sourcing of its CUDA alternative could erode Nvidia's software moat at the exact moment export controls are pushing China toward alternatives. And Xi's WAIC keynote reframed the US-China AI competition as a choice between openness and gatekeeping — a pitch aimed squarely at the Global South.
sources: Tom's Hardware, CSIS, The Verge, SCMP, Nikkei Asia
